exam: Certified Kubernetes and Cloud Native Associate (KCNA 2025)
note: Questions 1, 2, 50 answers corrected based on OCR. Only questions visible in uploaded images are included.

questions.question_text: What are the two essential operations that the kube-scheduler normally performs?
questions.explanation: kube-scheduler works in two phases: Filtering (finds nodes that meet Pod requirements) and Scoring (ranks eligible nodes to pick the best one).

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) in Kubernetes?

options.A: To manage the storage of persistent volumes within a Kubernetes cluster
options.B: To automatically scale pods based on resource usage
options.C: To define a set of custom resources that extend the Kubernetes API
options.D: To facilitate network communication between Pods and services

questions.explanation: CRDs extend the Kubernetes API by allowing users to define custom resource types beyond built-in ones like Pods and Services.

questions.question_text: A Kubernetes deployment must run only on nodes with specific hardware capabilities. What configuration should be used to ensure that Pods are scheduled only on those nodes?

options.A: Use Pod affinity rules in the deployment configuration
options.B: Configure resource limits and requests for the Pods
options.C: Use a nodeSelector in the deployment specification
options.D: Set a PodDisruptionBudget for the deployment

questions.explanation: nodeSelector is the simplest way to constrain a Pod to nodes with specific labels. It matches key-value labels assigned to nodes.

questions.question_text: What are the possible options for PolicyTypes when configuring NetworkPolicy?
questions.explanation: NetworkPolicy supports two PolicyTypes: ingress (controls incoming traffic) and egress (controls outgoing traffic).

questions.question_text: What is the main role of CNCF Ambassadors in the cloud native ecosystem?

options.A: They manage project governance by guiding development, reviewing code, and approving changes
options.B: They handle CNCF operations by managing budgets, securing sponsors, and negotiating agreements
options.C: They design Kubernetes features by writing code, planning releases, and coordinating development
options.D: They promote cloud native adoption by supporting communities, organizing events, and advocating for CNCF projects

questions.explanation: CNCF Ambassadors are community advocates who promote cloud native adoption through events, education, and community support.

questions.question_text: Which Kubernetes feature is the simplest way to force a Pod or Deployment to run only on nodes with a specific label?
questions.explanation: NodeSelector is the simplest mechanism for node selection based on labels. NodeAffinity is more advanced but more complex.

questions.question_text: CNCF supports and helps organize different Kubernetes related events: meetups, KubeCons, KubeDays, KCDs etc. What does KCD stand for?
questions.explanation: KCD stands for Kubernetes Community Days - local community-organized events supported by CNCF.

questions.question_text: Which is the correct kubectl command to display logs in real time?
questions.explanation: The -f flag means 'follow', which streams logs in real time, similar to 'tail -f' in Linux.

questions.question_text: A Pod needs to be configured for a slow-starting application. What is the best approach to ensure the Pod is only marked as ready when it is fully initialized?

options.A: Use both readiness and startup probes
options.B: Use both liveness and startup probes

questions.explanation: For slow-starting applications, a startup probe delays liveness checks until the app is ready. Combined with a liveness probe, it ensures the container stays healthy after startup.

questions.question_text: DNS issues are suspected inside a Pod. Which command is most useful to verify the problem?

options.A: kubectl delete pod to recreate the Pod and test if the issue resolves after restarting
options.B: kubectl logs <pod> to review application output for errors related to DNS resolution
options.C: kubectl exec <pod> -- nslookup <service-name> to check name resolution directly inside the Pod
options.D: kubectl get endpoints to confirm whether Services have the correct backend addresses

questions.explanation: Running nslookup inside the Pod directly tests DNS resolution from the Pod's network namespace, which is the most accurate way to debug DNS issues.

questions.question_text: In a Kubernetes cluster, how are Labels and Selectors utilized to manage and organize resources?

options.A: Labels are used for categorizing resources, while Selectors are used to filter and retrieve resources based on those labels
options.B: Labels are automatically generated by Kubernetes, and Selectors are manually defined by users
options.C: Selectors can only be applied to Pods and not to other resource types in Kubernetes
options.D: Labels are used only for service discovery and do not affect resource management

questions.explanation: Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects for categorization. Selectors use those labels to filter and select specific groups of resources.

questions.question_text: Which Kubernetes resource provides standardized load balancing, TLS termination, and multi-context traffic routing across different implementations?
questions.explanation: Gateway API is the modern, extensible successor to Ingress that provides standardized load balancing, TLS termination, and advanced traffic routing across implementations.

questions.question_text: A Pod was created that shows the ErrImagePull status. What is most the cause of the error?

options.A: The container image cannot be downloaded
options.B: Kube-scheduler cannot find a node to execute the Pod
options.C: Kubelet is not running properly on the node
options.D: The container image needs to run using hostNetwork

questions.explanation: ErrImagePull means Kubernetes failed to pull the container image from the registry. Causes include wrong image name, tag, or missing registry credentials.

questions.question_text: How can a Pod be configured to use a ServiceAccount for authenticated API access?

options.A: By converting the ServiceAccount into a ConfigMap that the Pod consumes as environment variables
options.B: By attaching the ServiceAccount directly to the kube-scheduler to change how the Pod is scheduled
options.C: By embedding the ServiceAccount token inside the container image at build time
options.D: By specifying the ServiceAccount name in the Pod specs, Kubernetes mounts its token as a volume

questions.explanation: When a ServiceAccount name is specified in the Pod spec, Kubernetes automatically mounts its token as a volume, enabling the Pod to authenticate with the API server.

questions.question_text: What command would be used to check the revisions of Deployment web-app?
questions.explanation: kubectl rollout history shows the revision history of a Deployment, including previous versions and change causes.

questions.question_text: Which command correctly uses kubectl exec to inspect environment variables inside a running container?
questions.explanation: The 'env' command lists all environment variables inside the container. kubectl exec -- env runs it directly in the container.

questions.question_text: What is the primary role of a Service in a Kubernetes cluster?

options.A: To manage the scaling of applications automatically in the cluster
options.B: It exposes an application running behind a single outward-facing endpoint
options.C: To store configuration data and secrets in the cluster
options.D: To provide an endpoint for outward side to access the cluster on the Internet

questions.explanation: A Kubernetes Service provides a stable network endpoint to access a group of Pods, abstracting away their individual IPs which can change.

questions.question_text: Which of the following Pod Security Standards enforces the most restrictive settings, designed for security-critical workloads?
questions.explanation: The Restricted policy enforces the most security-hardened settings, following current Pod hardening best practices for security-critical workloads.

questions.question_text: A Kubernetes Pod is returning a CrashLoopBackOff status. What is the most likely reason for this behavior?

options.A: There are insufficient resources allocated for the Pod
options.B: The Pod is unable to communicate with the Kubernetes API server
options.C: The application inside the container crashed after starting
options.D: The container's image is missing or cannot be pulled

questions.explanation: CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes keeps restarting it. The most common cause is the application inside crashing after startup.

questions.question_text: A logging agent must be deployed to run on every node in a Kubernetes cluster. Which resource should be used to achieve this requirement?
questions.explanation: A DaemonSet ensures that a copy of a Pod runs on every (or selected) node in the cluster, making it ideal for node-level logging or monitoring agents.

questions.question_text: Which set of resources can receive automatic updates from the HorizontalPodAutoscaler?
questions.explanation: HorizontalPodAutoscaler can scale Deployments and StatefulSets automatically based on CPU/memory metrics or custom metrics.

questions.question_text: Which command updates the image of a running deployment without recreating the resource?

options.D: kubectl set image deployment/my-app rollout restart

questions.explanation: kubectl set image updates the container image of an existing deployment in-place, triggering a rolling update without recreating the resource.

questions.question_text: Which statement is correct about Role and ClusterRole objects in Kubernetes?

options.A: Roles can only set permissions for resources within a particular namespace
options.B: ClusterRoles and Roles can remove permissions from resources within a namespace
options.C: ClusterRoles can only set permissions for resources that are not assigned to a namespace
options.D: Roles can set permissions for both namespaced and non-namespaced objects

questions.explanation: ClusterRoles apply cluster-wide and can grant permissions to non-namespaced resources. Roles are scoped to a specific namespace.

questions.question_text: In a Kubernetes cluster, how does DNS resolution work for services?

options.A: DNS resolution for services uses the IP addresses of the Pods directly
options.B: Kubernetes creates service names that resolve to stable cluster IPs
options.C: Service names in Kubernetes are resolved using environment variables set in each Pod
options.D: DNS resolution for services is handled by an external DNS provider that manages all service names

questions.explanation: CoreDNS in Kubernetes creates DNS records for Services that resolve to their stable ClusterIP, allowing Pods to reach services by name.

questions.question_text: In Kubernetes, how do Namespaces differ from Labels in managing resources within a cluster?

options.A: Namespaces are used to isolate resources, while Labels are used for query and selection
options.B: Namespaces are assigned statically, while Labels can be modified dynamically
options.C: Namespaces are only used for access control, while Labels are solely for resource monitoring
options.D: Namespaces can only contain Pods, but Labels can be applied to any Kubernetes resource

questions.explanation: Namespaces provide resource isolation and scope, while Labels are flexible metadata used for querying, selecting, and grouping resources.

questions.question_text: Prometheus has 4 core metric types: Summary, Histogram, Gauge. What is the 4th metric type?
questions.explanation: The four Prometheus metric types are: Counter, Gauge, Histogram, and Summary. Counter is a monotonically increasing value.

questions.question_text: How can a tls-type secret be created that holds the TLS certificate and its associated key using the kubectl command?
questions.explanation: The correct syntax uses 'secret tls' type with --from-file flags specifying cert and key paths explicitly.

questions.question_text: A developer is trying to understand the concept of containerization for their Kubernetes deployment. What best describes containerization?

options.A: A way to create and manage virtual machines for running various applications
options.B: A lightweight approach to running applications in isolated environments using shared OS resources
options.C: A technique that allows applications to run only on bare metal servers without any abstraction
options.D: A method of virtualizing physical hardware to run multiple operating systems

questions.explanation: Containerization packages applications with their dependencies in isolated environments that share the host OS kernel, making them lightweight compared to VMs.

questions.question_text: In a Kubernetes environment, how is an Argo CD ApplicationSet configured to ensure continuous deployment of an application?

options.A: By manually deploying the application using kubectl commands every time changes are made
options.B: By using a Helm chart to package the application and deploying it directly without GitOps
options.C: By defining the ApplicationSet in a YAML file and linking it to a Git repository containing application manifests
options.D: By defining the ApplicationSet containing deployment parameters and applying it with kubectl

questions.explanation: Argo CD ApplicationSet uses GitOps - it watches a Git repository and automatically syncs the cluster state with the declared manifests in the repo.

questions.question_text: The Kubernetes Administration Team created several new nodes with SSD Disks and labeled them with: <node-name> disktype=ssd. Which of the following Pod manifests makes sure the Pod will be placed only in one of these specifically labeled nodes?
questions.explanation: The correct YAML syntax for nodeSelector uses key: value format (disktype: ssd), which matches the node label disktype=ssd.

questions.question_text: In a Kubernetes cluster, the component called etcd plays an essential role. What is the primary function of etcd?

options.A: To replicate cluster state data across control plane nodes for high availability
options.B: To provide a distributed key-value store for all cluster state and data
options.C: To maintain the desired state of workloads by reconciling changes through controllers
options.D: To persist API object definitions such as Pods, ConfigMaps, and Secrets

questions.explanation: etcd is a distributed key-value store that Kubernetes uses as its backing store for all cluster data, including configuration and state.

questions.question_text: By default, Pods are non-isolated and accept traffic from any source. When a NetworkPolicy selects a Pod, it becomes isolated. How are multiple NetworkPolicies for a Pod handled in Kubernetes?
questions.explanation: Multiple NetworkPolicies affecting the same Pod are additive - the union of all their rules is applied. There is no conflict or hierarchy.

questions.question_text: How are ConfigMaps correctly used in Kubernetes applications?

options.A: A ConfigMap is used to provide configuration values that Pods consume as files or variables
options.B: A ConfigMap is used to keep encrypted secrets such as passwords for databases or tokens
options.C: A ConfigMap is used to hold shared log files that applications produce during execution
options.D: A ConfigMap is used to adjust cluster scheduling rules applied by the Kubernetes scheduler

questions.explanation: ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs that can be injected into Pods as environment variables or mounted as files.

questions.question_text: What is the correct command to scale multiple Pods for a deployment?
questions.explanation: kubectl scale --replicas=N is the correct syntax to scale a Deployment to N replicas (Pods).

questions.question_text: Lightweight network proxies such as Envoy are part of which plane in a service mesh?
questions.explanation: Sidecar proxies like Envoy operate in the data plane, handling actual traffic between services. The control plane (e.g., Istio) configures these proxies.

questions.question_text: A Kubernetes node is on hardware that is scheduled for maintenance. How can the Pods scheduled on that node be identified so workloads can be redirected before the node leaves service?
questions.explanation: kubectl get pods --on <nodename> lists all pods running on a specific node, helping identify workloads to be redirected before maintenance.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a challenge derived from running cloud native applications across multiple cloud providers?

options.A: The cost optimization is complex to maintain across different public cloud environments
options.B: The operational costs of maintaining the data center of the company
options.C: The lack of services provided by the most common public clouds
options.D: The lack of different container images available in public image repositories

questions.explanation: Multi-cloud environments introduce complexity in cost management due to different pricing models, billing systems, and resource types across providers.

questions.question_text: Which statement is true about the Container Storage Interface (CSI) in Kubernetes?

options.A: CSI provides storage for workloads by focusing on NFS and iSCSI backends and offering basic volume provisioning
options.B: CSI introduces a model where applications mount storage by referencing the driver name in the Pod specification instead of using PVs
options.C: CSI is a Kubernetes-only API built into the kubelet; drivers are compiled in-tree and selected directly in a PersistentVolume
options.D: CSI is a cross-platform specification; in Kubernetes, CSI drivers run as pods with sidecars and are consumed through StorageClass and PersistentVolume

questions.explanation: CSI is an industry-standard specification. In Kubernetes, CSI drivers are deployed as Pods with sidecar containers and integrated via StorageClass and PersistentVolume objects.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes observability in a cloud native environment?

options.A: Observability is the process of restarting failed Pods to maintain application availability
options.B: Observability is the configuration of health probes to check whether containers are running correctly
options.C: Observability is the use of alerts and dashboards to notify administrators about system outages
options.D: Observability is the practice of collecting and analyzing logs, metrics, and traces to understand system behavior

questions.explanation: Observability in cloud native is the ability to understand system internals through its external outputs: logs, metrics, and traces (the three pillars).

questions.question_text: Which of the following common practices will be an antipattern when the GitOps workflow goes live?

options.A: Deploying Kubernetes objects via imperative commands
options.B: Pushing builds with the deploy label
options.C: Deploying Kubernetes objects via declarative manifests
options.D: Leaving manual comments in the PR comments forum

questions.explanation: GitOps requires declarative configuration stored in Git. Using imperative commands bypasses Git as the source of truth, making it an antipattern.

questions.question_text: Which statement describes the purpose of Labels in Kubernetes?

options.A: Labels provide globally unique identifiers for all objects across the cluster
options.B: Labels must be defined on objects before they can be created in Kubernetes
options.C: Labels assign objects to specific namespaces to support resource isolation
options.D: Labels are used to attach identifying metadata to objects so they can be selected and grouped

questions.explanation: Labels are arbitrary key-value metadata attached to Kubernetes objects, used by selectors to group, filter, and manage resources.

questions.question_text: If a Pod contains multiple containers, how can logs be viewed from one of the containers?
questions.explanation: The --container flag specifies which container's logs to retrieve in a multi-container Pod.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum of etcd members that are required for a highly available Kubernetes cluster?
questions.explanation: etcd requires a quorum of (n/2)+1 members. Three members is the minimum for HA, allowing one member to fail while maintaining quorum.

questions.question_text: A platform engineer is tasked with ensuring that an application can securely access the Kubernetes API without using a developer's personal credentials. What is the correct way to achieve this?

options.A: Set the application to use the default ServiceAccount in the namespace
options.B: Generate a certificate for the application to access the API
options.C: Use a developer's kubeconfig file with restricted permissions
options.D: Create a ServiceAccount and bind it to the Pod for API access

questions.explanation: Creating a dedicated ServiceAccount and binding it to the Pod with appropriate RBAC roles is the secure, recommended way for applications to access the Kubernetes API.

questions.question_text: In a Kubernetes cluster, which component is responsible for maintaining the desired state of applications by reconciling resources such as Deployments?
questions.explanation: The Controller Manager runs controllers that watch the state of the cluster and make changes to move the current state towards the desired state.

questions.question_text: Which cloud native tool(s) keeps Kubernetes clusters in sync with sources of configuration (like Git repositories), and automates updates to configuration when there is a change in Git?
questions.explanation: Flux and Argo CD are the leading GitOps tools that automatically sync Kubernetes cluster state with Git repository configurations.

questions.question_text: In Kubernetes Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), what is the primary difference between a Role and a ClusterRole?

options.A: A Role is namespace-scoped and applies only within a single namespace, while a ClusterRole is cluster-scoped and applies across all namespaces
options.B: A Role is limited to granting access to Pods and Services, while a ClusterRole can grant access to every type of Kubernetes resource
options.C: A Role defines permissions for modifying resources, while a ClusterRole defines permissions only for viewing resources across the cluster
options.D: A Role is generally weaker in scope and power, while a ClusterRole is always considered stronger and more powerful than a Role

questions.explanation: Roles are namespace-scoped RBAC objects. ClusterRoles are cluster-scoped and can also be used to grant access to cluster-wide resources.

questions.question_text: Which Pod Security Standard balances ease of adoption while still preventing known privilege escalations?
questions.explanation: Baseline prevents known privilege escalations while minimizing restrictions, balancing security and ease of adoption. Restricted is the most strict.

questions.question_text: What does the livenessProbe in Kubernetes help detect?

options.B: When a container has started successfully
options.C: When a container is ready to serve traffic
options.D: When a container exceeds resource limits

questions.explanation: livenessProbe checks if a container is still running/healthy. If it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container. readinessProbe checks if it is ready to serve traffic.

questions.question_text: A platform engineer wants to ensure that a new microservice is automatically deployed to every cluster registered in Argo CD. Which configuration best achieves this?

options.A: Create an Argo CD ApplicationSet that uses a Git repository containing the microservice manifests
options.B: Set up a Kubernetes CronJob that redeploys the microservice to all registered clusters on a schedule
options.C: Manually configure every registered cluster with the deployment YAML for installing the microservice
options.D: Use a Helm chart to package the microservice and manage it with a single Application defined in ArgoCD

questions.explanation: An ApplicationSet in Argo CD generates Applications for multiple clusters automatically from a single Git repository definition.

questions.question_text: Gateway API is a successor to what Kubernetes API?
questions.explanation: Gateway API is designed as the next generation successor to the Ingress API, offering more expressive and extensible traffic routing capabilities.

questions.question_text: A DevOps engineer is preparing worker-1 for maintenance. The node is running multiple Pods, but workloads must be safely evicted and rescheduled. Which command should the engineer use?
questions.explanation: kubectl drain safely evicts all Pods from a node and marks it unschedulable. --ignore-daemonsets skips DaemonSet-managed Pods which cannot be evicted normally.

questions.question_text: Which CNCF project is primarily used as a high-performance proxy for microservices, handling service to service communication?
questions.explanation: Envoy is a CNCF graduated project used as a high-performance L7 proxy and communication bus for cloud native applications and service meshes.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains the Container Storage Interface (CSI) concept in Kubernetes?

options.A: CSI is a networking interface that manages container-to-container communication across nodes
options.B: CSI is a standard API specification that allows Kubernetes (and other orchestrators) to use different storage systems via plugins
options.C: CSI is a framework inside Kubernetes that automatically provisions local node storage without requiring external storage plugins
options.D: CSI is a Kubernetes-only API that provides built-in storage drivers for Pods to access persistent volumes

questions.explanation: CSI is an industry-standard interface that decouples storage providers from Kubernetes, allowing any storage system to be used via a plugin/driver.

questions.question_text: What happens if a container runtime is missing on Kubernetes worker nodes?

options.A: Pods can not communicate with each other
options.B: Nodes can not communicate with each other
options.D: kubelet can not communicate with kube-api-server

questions.explanation: The container runtime (e.g., containerd, CRI-O) is required by kubelet to create and manage containers. Without it, kubelet cannot launch any Pods.

questions.question_text: Which Kubernetes Service type exposes a service only within the cluster?
questions.explanation: ClusterIP is the default Service type. It assigns a virtual IP only accessible within the cluster, not externally.

questions.question_text: A Kubernetes administrator wants to limit the total memory usage of all Pods in a specific namespace to 4Gi. What should they create?
questions.explanation: ResourceQuota sets aggregate limits on total resource consumption (CPU, memory, object count) within a namespace.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of an Ingress resource in Kubernetes?

options.A: To expose internal services to external web traffic
options.B: To manage persistent volumes for application data
options.C: To store configuration data used by applications
options.D: To monitor the health status of application Pods

questions.explanation: Ingress manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to services within a cluster, providing load balancing, SSL termination, and name-based virtual hosting.

questions.question_text: Which file in a Helm chart is responsible for defining the default configuration values that can be overridden during deployment?
questions.explanation: values.yaml contains default configuration values for a Helm chart. These can be overridden at install/upgrade time using --set or -f flags.
